item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i was about to say, "this feels like one of those weird Lost Episode creepypastas about a show that doesn't actually exist" before i remembered what site i was on.

and by "creepypasta," i mean the good ones, like "Candle Cove." +1

(PS why did the ending remind me of St Elsewhere)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone else who didn't even think of furries while reading through this scip I have to give this a +1. To me it was a story of a lonely person who managed to literally 'make friends' from his dogs using anomalous technology. The comment in episode 14 and the ending made me feel sincerely sad that the scip got neutralised by probable malfunction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one who did not think about furries? This is pretty sad, so good job.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One or several images in this article have been removed due to non-compliance with the Creative Commons license. They may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact apoplexic or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.


This automated message was sent on behalf of apoplexic. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact apoplexic in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: Necro post
item.content:encoded:
I saw it more as a "device that morphs them into humanoids" than any sort of splicing deal.
If the device shorted out while something was being affected, I want to assume that the single living specimen was the last alive before the device was fully destroyed/rendered inactive, and it was stuck in the middle of reverting back to normal.
The furry fantasy idea kinda ruined the skip for me, but +1 all the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so much better without the picture. (Given that's why I apparently never upvoted.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whether this is intended to be a furry joke on some level or not is irrelevant to me. This is a really wonderfully tragic SCP. This is one I read about a year ago (I was surprised to find this was written in 2012, and I was in fact combing Series III trying to find it before I thought to search the "canine" tag), and it's one that stayed with me. It's haunting and sad and beautiful. I really like this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps because he couldn't afford it. I think more than the last episode correlates with real life, such as, the episode in which 'Lad' cannot find a job. Perhaps this is more like reality TV than a staged sitcom.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
From Page 2:

I'll just make a note here: Darkwolf, "Neutralised" is not a mis-spelling of "Neutralized", it's just a Britishism.

Don't fix UK spellings of a word to the American version under "spelling errors". However, from what I can find, Dalmation is indeed a spelling error, unless someone can say otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus Christ. This article reminded me a lot of the works of ShayeSaintJohn. Cheap, gaudy, hells of unsettling and above all batshit insane. Please, just take my upvote.
Bark… I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, as both a furry and the article's author, would like to state that was in no way the intent. If anything, the notion that this is fantasy fulfillment is closer to the actual case, though neither actually are. It's based on my observations as someone who finds canines adorable, but is not connected to them more substantially than that.

However, if something in the article irritated you in some fashion, feel absolutely free to downvote for that. Whatever it may be, and whether or not I disagree with you, if something about the article makes it seem below acceptable in terms of quality for you, a downvote is the appropiate action. Your are free to rate the article as you please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To beat a dead horse, you can count me as another furry who enjoys this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This made perfect sense to me on first reading without need for comments.

I like this a lot, it's tragic without being maudlin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a furry, this just feels like a retarded reason for a downvote.
For reference, the author is a furry too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, too, downvote articles that hurt my delicate feelings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm curious as to your reasoning behind why this is "furry bashing". If this is bashing it, then what's SCP-953 to you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a furry, this just feels like furry-bashing to me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you mean "tragedy". And to use capital letters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like it, i dont think e was splicing them at all. he found something that let them be humanoid for however long and when it broke they didnt change back, but couldnt stay sapient. tragicness all over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My heart fucking stopped when I opened that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture creeped me out, I know it didn't for other people, but I had never seen it before. And the article was creepy as shit to me. Like someone else said, it doesn't make sense (at least without the author explaining it), and it's just very…wtf-ish. It's weird, and the footage of malnourished dogs threw me off big-time from anything I thought it could have been. I don't know, I like this. I think what the author disclosed did detract from it a bit, but taking the article on its own terms, it's really freaking me out.

Oh yeah, is anyone going to remove the footnotes? Because at least for the one giving the number of episodes, couldn't you just put "multiple episodes (15)" or something like that in the body of the article to do away with the footnotes? And as for the other footnote, we learn the man's name is Lad in the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, I've updated this to make it somewhat more anomalous and worth investigation. Hopefully that sorts the previous issue out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why did the guy die, though? If he was still sapient, he could've just gone out and bought food for himself and his dogs. That's why I think something must've happened to affect him, too.

The humanoid dog actually makes perfect sense. The real mystery is why the other dogs didn't look humanoid anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well played, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm only slightly disturbed that I was pretty familiar with the picture before this article. That being said, it's vague-ish and creepy-ish enough. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am currently working on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No one would ever realistically conclude that the broadcast came from no source.
The FCC (I think they would be the agency in charge) would start investigating an illegal broadcast, and attempt to triangulate the source. Depending on how you envision the anomaly working, they would either not be able to determine the location, get several different and irregular sources, or get a location and then find no trasmitter there. In either case, they would conclude that the pirates are using a mobile transmiter and/or some slick technology tricks. A frustrating and open-ended case for the agency, but nothing out of the ordinary to ping The Foundations radar.

I think it might work better if you inserted some detail that would make the broadcast weird from the get-go. Like, maybe it isn't in a TV signal format, something that should just come across as static. That way the Foundation would have a plausible reason to start paying attention, and then they could find out the sourceless-ness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something I could probably do with making a little more explicit: the broadcast comes from nowhere. It has no sensical point of origin.

I'll have a look at editing the article to make this more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did the Foundation determine that the broadcast was anomalous and hence needed to be blocked? Were the anthropomorphic dogs too realistic to be done by makeup and/or special effects? It could be done in theory, but someone doing a pirate broadcast wouldn't have the money to do it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy $*%^ing dogs&/*ing f$#%balls of creepy
Don't change the first picture. All the stuff that doesn't make sense, doesn't make sense in just the right way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do resent the implication that I can't approach a subject matter like this semi-objectively just because of whatever proclivities I might have. I wouldn't assume a heterosexual man writing about sex was gulty of author-insert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it can be furry wish fulfillment (which it is) without sex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think we might be at a cross-definition of "furry", which tends to happen with slang terms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm someone who is usually inclined to take the same stance as you, Jekeled. It's the reason why I still prefer to think of SCP-1782 as an instance of a Lynchian nightmare room incapable of being comprehended rather than what Faminepulse intended it to be (though I still think Faminepulse's explanation is really neat and creepy on its own).

Yet before reading the discussion for this SCP, I happened to form an interpretation that matched up almost exactly with Bunton's. So this ended up being one of the few times it didn't really bother me as much as it usually would. And I'd imagine that there were plenty of people in the discussion for both this page and for SCP-1782 who felt more satisfied that the authors gave explanations of what they were trying to achieve.

I guess the lesson to be learned for everyone here is to try and find a happy median between explaining so little that people are left aggravated out of confusion and so much that the mystique of the article gets diminished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, you can always have your headcanon for the SCP, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems to me like the author of the broadcast was the dead guy, but then, the mystery is with the humanoid dog.

Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this so much. It is because what could be the backstory of it all. I can imagine… a guy inventing some kind of a gadget in a camera that somehow sees the dogs as human, living beings, which is destroyed in Episode 14 during some kind of a storm that maybe damaged the gadget during the shoot? Silly? Maybe. But this SCP gave me that implication. It's great.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't really sure. That's why I liked it so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, I thought it's a picture of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god, it's a picture of me…

item.title: Re: Hurricane date nitpick
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I just made up a date. It's a piece of fiction made by sketchy people, so it would be odd for it to be so attentive to facts.

item.title: Re: Hurricane date nitpick
item.content:encoded: Touche. I just realized the "show" is set in Los Angeles, which gets hurricanes even less frequently than Canada.

item.title: Re: Hurricane date nitpick
item.content:encoded: It's pretty clear that Canada usually doesn't get hurricanes. Who's to say what happens in the show is really what happened?

item.title: Hurricane date nitpick
item.content:encoded:
The given date in May is far too early for hurricane season and completely broke disbelief for me. Same thing with the discovery in Canada… I know they can experience hurricanes, but why not leave it in Texas?

This Wikipedia article may help if you want to tie in to a specific, real-world storm. Alternatively, you could just partially blackbox the date but pick a month in hurricane season.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_Atlantic_hurricane_season

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it was probably because it was coming from an unknown source with unknown people working on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And yet there was nothing sexual at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It certainly isn't uncommon, especially on this wiki. But don't get me wrong, I'm not saying that's automatically a bad thing: SCP-173, the original SCP, has the same basic "gimmick" as the Weeping Angels, SCP-055 is somewhat similar to the Silence, and SCP-1678 is another in a fairly long line of "supernatural cities underneath London," but they (along with this article) have plenty of unique twists on their underlying concepts to be considered distinct.

This SCP's similarity to Rabbits is actually one of the reasons I upvoted. I like me my David Lynch. Heck, just about everything that Lynch has ever done is a potential oil gusher for SCP fuel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well done and creepy as hell, but it seems weird that the Foundation would have it tagged as a SCP before they knew about the hybridization thing. The broadcast was strange, sure, but it wasn't threatening normalcy. For all they knew, it could have been some people in fursuits. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. I'd been unfamiliar with that up until now. Interesting stuff.

Mrah. It's not uncommon for more than one person to come to vaguely similiar ideas independantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, an eerie "sitcom" that features furries who are portrayed as spouting semi-sensical, occasionally ominous dialogue? and with a storm figuring into the "plot" of the show?

Sounds a bit like Rabbits, except less creepy and more saddening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll just make a note here: Darkwolf, "Neutralised" is not a mis-spelling of "Neutralized", it's just a Britishism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My take on it was that the backlash from the device breaking caused the human to revert to an unintelligent animalistic state too. I'm not sure that was the author's intent, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Little Hound on the Prarie, Law and Rover, How I Fetched Your Mother, The Big Bark Theory, uhh… hmm… I thought I would have more shows XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sadly, several porn sites come to mind. :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a thing of beauty

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but I'm biased. That first picture needs to go though. I don't think you can find a picture like that that isn't going to distract the reader.

This is a concept I would have said wouldn't work, but the execution and ending saves it. I like the idea of the item breaking and causing some sort of backlash. Also, while this is sort of furry wish fulfillment, there's important element in that the guy himself isn't a furry. It makes it less wanting to be a furry and more just being lonely and wanting friends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, where else would I find images of people dressing up as dogs?
